title: Charlie Ewels hit on Sexton — England vs Ireland 2022
explanation: Ewels makes direct contact with the head of Sexton with no mitigation. Upright body position, no wrap, direct to the head. Straight red card under the Head Contact Process.

title: Valetini red card for head clash
explanation: Head-on-head contact in the tackle. Direct contact to the head with high degree of danger. The tackler has a duty of care regardless of intent. Red card.

title: De Allende red card incident
explanation: De Allende makes contact with the head of the ball carrier. Under the Head Contact Process: direct contact to the head, at least medium degree of danger. Red card.

title: Cheslin Kolbe challenge — South Africa vs Ireland 2022
explanation: Kolbe's challenge is reviewed by TMO. Low force and passive movement are mitigating factors that reduce the sanction from red to yellow. A borderline call — good training for understanding mitigation.

title: Red card — England vs Ireland U20 Championship
explanation: A clear high tackle in the U20 World Championship. Contact above the shoulders with force. The Head Contact Process applies at all age grades. Red card.

title: Anelisa Mteto dangerous tackle — RugbyTown 7s 2022
explanation: A dangerous tackle in women's 7s rugby. The laws on dangerous tackles apply equally in sevens and 15s, men's and women's. Yellow card issued for the dangerous technique.

title: Digby spear tackle
explanation: A spear/tip tackle where the ball carrier is lifted and driven into the ground head-first. This is one of the most dangerous acts in rugby. Automatic red card — there is no mitigation for a tip tackle.

title: Rory Sidey lift tackle — legal or illegal?
explanation: A lifting tackle where the ball carrier is tipped past horizontal. Even if the landing is not head-first, tipping a player beyond horizontal is dangerous. The referee must assess the degree of danger — was the player driven into the ground or placed down?

title: George Kruis sin bin — dump tackle on Halaifonua
explanation: Kruis lifts and dumps the ball carrier. A dump tackle where the player is lifted and driven into the ground. The referee assesses the degree of danger: was there a tip? How did the player land? Yellow card in this case.

title: Reckless headbutt in the ruck
explanation: A deliberate headbutt in the ruck. This is clear off-the-ball foul play — intentional contact with the head using the head. Straight red card, no mitigation possible for deliberate acts of violence.

title: TMO confirms penalty try and Watson yellow — NatWest 6 Nations
explanation: The defending player commits a professional foul preventing a probable try. TMO reviews and confirms penalty try. Yellow card — the infringement was cynical and directly prevented a probable try.

title: Kwagga Smith incident — South Africa vs Argentina 2022
explanation: Professional foul near the try line. A try would probably have been scored but for the cynical infringement. Penalty try awarded plus yellow card for the offending player.

title: Frisby penalty try — controversial call
explanation: The scrum-half's actions are deemed a professional foul preventing a probable try. Controversial because it required careful assessment of whether a try would probably have been scored without the infringement.

title: Penalty try — law implementation guide
explanation: An educational example of when a penalty try should be awarded. A penalty try is given when a try would probably have been scored if not for foul play by the defending team. It is always awarded under the posts.

title: Tackler not releasing — Law 15.4(a)
explanation: The tackler does not release the tackled player after bringing them to ground. Law 15: the tackler must release the ball carrier and move away. Clear penalty.

title: Legal vs illegal sealing off — The Rugby Pros
explanation: This clip compares legal and illegal body positions over the ball. Going off your feet and lying on or over the ball ('sealing off') prevents fair contest. The key question: is the player supporting their own body weight?

title: Off your feet — Wayne Barnes explains
explanation: Former World Rugby referee Wayne Barnes explains the difference between positive play and going off your feet at the breakdown. Players arriving at the ruck must stay on their feet and support their own body weight.

title: Supporting body weight / hands on the floor — Wayne Barnes
explanation: Wayne Barnes demonstrates the fine line between legal and illegal positions at the breakdown. Having hands on the floor does not automatically mean 'off feet' — the question is whether the player is supporting their own body weight.

title: The jackler — legal turnover technique
explanation: A demonstration of legal jackaling technique. The defender arrives before the ruck forms, stays on their feet, and gets hands on the ball. This is excellent defensive play — not a penalty. Referees must reward good technique.

title: Hardest clean & legal tackles in rugby — compilation
explanation: A massive but completely legal hit. Arms wrapped, contact below the shoulders, driving through. Big does not mean illegal — this is textbook tackling. Play on.

title: Legal tackle #2 — huge but clean
explanation: Another thunderous but perfectly legal tackle. Full arm wrap, contact point below the shoulders. Don't penalise physicality when the technique is correct.

title: Legal tackle #3 — dominant defence
explanation: A dominant defensive tackle that sends the ball carrier backwards. Looks dramatic but technique is sound. Arms wrapped, legal contact point. Reward good defence by letting play continue.

title: Legal tackle #4 — is this too high?
explanation: This tackle looks borderline but is legal. Initial contact point is on the chest/shoulder area, below the line of the shoulders. The ball carrier's height change makes it look higher than it is. Play on.

title: Massive legal tackle example — RugbyPass
explanation: RugbyPass example of a massive but legal tackle. Initial contact below the shoulders, tackler uses arms to wrap. Impressive physicality within the laws.

title: Legal tackle vs illegal lifting tackle — The Rugby Pros
explanation: The Rugby Pros compare a legal tackle with an illegal lifting tackle. A tackle becomes illegal when the ball carrier is lifted and tipped past the horizontal. A legal tackle can involve a lift as long as the player is brought down safely.

title: The knock-on and forward pass explained
explanation: A clear explanation of what constitutes a knock-on. The ball must travel forward from the hands or arms towards the opposing dead-ball line. Results in a scrum to the non-offending team.

title: Forward pass example
explanation: A clear forward pass — the ball travels forward out of the passer's hands. Remember: judged by direction from the hands, not where it's caught (momentum can carry it). Scrum to the non-offending team.

title: Offside penalty conceded in open play
explanation: A player in front of the offside line interferes with play. The offside line at a ruck is the hindmost foot of the last player bound in the ruck. Penalty.

title: Offside from a kick — players advancing
explanation: Players from the kicking team who are ahead of the kicker must retire and not advance until played onside. These players are advancing towards the ball — penalty for offside from a kick.

title: Obstruction — running for the ball
explanation: A player obstructs an opponent who is trying to play the ball. Obstruction includes blocking, shielding the ball carrier, or running a line that prevents a defender from making a tackle. Penalty.

title: When is advantage played? — The Rugby Pros
explanation: The referee plays advantage when the non-offending team may gain a tactical or territorial benefit. The advantage must be clear and real — if it doesn't materialise, play comes back for the original penalty.

title: Refereeing the scrum — England Rugby
explanation: England Rugby's guide to refereeing the scrum. Look for hinging, boring, angling, or collapsing. Scrum penalties are among the hardest calls — positioning and understanding the mechanics are crucial.

title: Offside at the ruck — Law 16.5(d)
explanation: Educational clip demonstrating offside at the ruck. Players not in the ruck must remain behind the hindmost foot of the last player bound in the ruck. Penalty.

title: 16 yellows and 4 reds — mass indiscipline
explanation: In a game with historic levels of indiscipline, the foul play is blatant. This clip shows clear dangerous play with no mitigation possible.

title: The most violent rugby match ever — what's the call?
explanation: Obvious off-the-ball foul play in an infamously violent match. Punching, stamping, or striking opponents away from the ball is always foul play. Red card for deliberate acts of violence.

title: Women's International — England vs France 2024 — early tackle
explanation: A strong tackle in women's international rugby. Good technique with arms wrapped below the shoulders. Physical but legal. Not every big collision requires a whistle.

title: Women's RWC — France vs England — early passage of play
explanation: An opening passage of play in the Women's Rugby World Cup. Tackles are legal, ball is released, rucks form correctly. No infringement in this phase — let the game flow.

title: Black Ferns vs USA 2025 — opening minutes
explanation: Clean rugby in the opening minutes of a women's international. Legal tackles, correct ruck formation, fair contest. Good, clean play should be rewarded by letting the game breathe.

title: How do referees decide between yellow and red? — The Rugby Pros
explanation: The Rugby Pros explain the Head Contact Process framework. The referee must assess: (1) Was there contact with the head? (2) What was the degree of danger? (3) Are there any mitigating factors? This systematic approach determines whether the sanction is penalty only, yellow, or red.

title: How does a referee decide to issue a red card? — The Rugby Pros
explanation: An in-depth look at the red card decision process. The threshold for red is: foul play + direct contact with the head + high degree of danger + no clear and obvious mitigation. Understanding this framework is essential for referee development.

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Off feet at the ruck
explanation: England concede a penalty for being off their feet at the breakdown. The arriving player goes to ground instead of staying on their feet and binding — a common breakdown offence under Law 15. Commentary confirms: 'England conceding the penalty off their feet.'

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Hands in the ruck + Yellow card
explanation: Cody Taylor plays the ball on the ground in the tackle area — a clear penalty. The TMO intervenes and upgrades the sanction: the referee awards a yellow card. Commentary: 'He plays it on the ground. A penalty to England.' then 'It's a yellow card.' Key learning: TMO can intervene to escalate sanctions for breakdown offences.

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Advantage into knock-on + TMO review
explanation: A contested breakdown situation. The referee initially sees a knock-on, but the TMO intervenes to check the detail. Commentary describes: 'Says he's seen a knock on at the breakdown.' The referee and TMO communicate to confirm the call. Good example of the TMO process and how officials work together.

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Offside at the lineout
explanation: England are offside at the lineout, which scrubs out a try. A massive turning point — the commentary confirms: 'England were offside at the lineout' and 'That is a big turnaround.' This is a great example of how offside at set pieces can have huge consequences. The knock-on became irrelevant once the penalty was awarded.

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Offside penalty, England under pressure
explanation: The referee spots an offside infringement after checking the big screen replay. England's number 4 is offside and concedes a penalty. Commentary: 'This is offside then' and 'England conceding the penalty.' A good example of how the referee uses available replays to make the right call.

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Not straight at the lineout
explanation: The lineout throw is not straight and New Zealand challenge. The referee awards the scrum to New Zealand. Commentary: 'Not straight. New Zealand were challenging. New Zealand ball.' Under Law 18, the ball must be thrown straight down the middle of the lineout.

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Scrum penalty under pressure
explanation: England are a man down (yellow card) and under pressure at the scrum. The scrum collapses and the referee awards a penalty to New Zealand. Commentary: 'It all looks messy. Penalty New Zealand.' A difficult call to read as scrums are chaotic, but the referee identifies the infringement.

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Knock-on from restart
explanation: A knock-on from the restart kick. The receiver jumps early and fails to gather cleanly. Commentary: 'Oh, he knocked it on. Knock on white.' Simple call — the ball goes forward off the hands. The referee awards a scrum.

title: All Blacks vs England 2025 — Advantage into penalty kick
explanation: England win a turnover penalty with just over six minutes to go. The referee plays advantage first before awarding the penalty. Commentary: 'England have a penalty. Could be a huge moment.' George Ford kicks for the posts to extend the lead. Key decision: when does the referee blow for the penalty vs. continue advantage?
